meta.source: Primary sources: NBER, FRB, CBO, OMB, OECD, BIS, IMF, MGI, PwC, Goldman Sachs, academic presses, primary press.

schema.magnitude_predicted: basis points or percent, null if qualitative

rows.quote: You can see the computer age everywhere but in the productivity statistics.
rows.context: Review of Manuel Castells' work, NYRB, July 1987.
rows.outcome_note: The foundational paradox statement. Resolved partially 1995-2004, returned 2004-2019.

rows.quote: The coming redesign of work will produce quantum jumps in productivity.
rows.context: HBR article on information work and productivity.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity growth remained subdued through the 1980s.

rows.quote: The post-industrial society will see services productivity rise through information technology.
rows.context: Introduction to paperback edition of The Coming of Post-Industrial Society.

rows.quote: How IT will change your life and raise productivity across the economy.
rows.context: Texas Monthly cover story on the personal computer revolution.
rows.outcome_note: The productivity boost did not arrive by 1990.

rows.quote: White-collar productivity growth has stalled despite massive IT investment.
rows.context: Morgan Stanley research note on service-sector productivity.

rows.quote: The productivity paradox of information technology: computers are everywhere but productivity growth has slowed.
rows.context: Communications of the ACM, December 1993. The academic framing of the paradox.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity acceleration began 1995-1996, roughly 2-3 years after the target.

rows.quote: No productivity boom on the horizon from information technology.
rows.context: Morgan Stanley research and congressional testimony.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity did boom starting 1995, shortly after the target year.

rows.quote: IT is unlikely to raise productivity growth permanently above the 1973-1990 baseline.
rows.context: NBER Working Paper 4549, measuring aggregate productivity consequences of IT.
rows.outcome_note: IT did raise productivity 1995-2004, but the effect proved temporary rather than permanent.

rows.quote: Long-run labor productivity growth assumption embedded in budget projections.
rows.context: CBO Economic and Budget Outlook, 1993. Assumed 1.3% labor productivity growth.

rows.quote: Economic assumptions for the FY1996 budget include accelerating productivity growth.
rows.context: FY1996 Budget, Economic Assumptions chapter. Embedded 1.5% productivity growth assumption.

rows.quote: The hollow ring of the productivity revival. IT spending does not translate to output.
The personal computer will transform office productivity within the next few years.
rows.context: The Road Ahead, 1995 (written 1994). Predicted near-term productivity gains from PC penetration.
rows.outcome_note: PC productivity gains arrived ~2000, 2 years after target.

rows.quote: The information revolution will fundamentally reshape business productivity by the turn of the century.
rows.context: Only the Paranoid Survive, 1996 (written 1994-95). Implied productivity transformation by 2000.

rows.quote: Computers and output growth revisited: the productivity contribution of IT will become visible.
IT-driven productivity revival by the late 1990s will validate equity valuations.
rows.context: Stocks for the Long Run, 1994. Predicted IT would raise productivity growth.

rows.quote: Long-run budget outlook assumes productivity growth of 1.25% annually.
Is there a tradeoff between unemployment and productivity growth? IT is unlikely to break the slowdown.
rows.context: NBER, 1995. Skepticism about IT's productivity ceiling.
rows.outcome_note: IT did boost productivity 1995-2004, but Gordon's broader skepticism about permanence was vindicated post-2004.

rows.quote: The business value of computers: IT spending does not correlate with productivity gains.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity did not rise by 1995; the boom came slightly later.

rows.quote: We are witnessing a pickup in productivity that appears to have roots in technological change.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity acceleration was visible almost immediately and persisted through 2004.

rows.quote: The pickup in productivity growth appears to be more than transitory.
Raising the speed limit: ICT will continue to contribute to productivity growth through the decade.
rows.context: Jorgenson & Stiroh, Brookings Papers on Economic Activity, 2000.
rows.outcome_note: ICT contribution faded after 2004; did not persist to 2010.

rows.quote: Information technology and the U.S. productivity revival: the gains are structural and will persist.
rows.outcome_note: Gains proved temporary; productivity growth slowed after 2004.

rows.quote: Beyond computation: information technology, organizational transformation and business performance.
rows.context: Journal of Economic Perspectives, Winter 2000. Productivity returns to IT visible by mid-2000s.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity returns were visible by early 2000s but did not persist to 2005.

rows.quote: Economic Report of the President assumes 1.5% labor productivity growth.
Economic Report of the President: productivity growth will remain elevated.
Economic Report assumes IT productivity acceleration will persist post-dot-com.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity growth slowed after 2004; the 1.75% assumption was not met.

rows.quote: America's computer and software industry will drive measurable productivity gains across sectors.
US productivity growth 1995-2000: the role of IT and the outlook for continued gains.
rows.context: MGI report, 2001. Predicted continued IT-driven productivity gains.

rows.quote: The resurgence of growth in the late 1990s: ICT contribution will persist.
Computers, obsolescence, and productivity: the IT contribution is structural.
Has the 'New Economy' rendered the productivity slowdown obsolete? No. The IT boom is temporary.
rows.context: NBER, 1999. Argued the IT productivity boom was temporary.
rows.outcome_note: Gordon was wrong about 1999-2004 but vindicated post-2004.

rows.quote: No productivity boom on the horizon from the internet.
Productivity in the services sector: cautious optimism that measurement improvements will reveal gains.
rows.outcome_note: Service-sector productivity gains were visible by early 2000s.

rows.quote: Do real-output and real-wage measures capture reality? Price-measurement gains will boost measured productivity.
rows.outcome_note: Price-measurement gains were real but smaller than projected; TFP slowed after 2004.

rows.quote: Is U.S. economic growth over? The death of innovation, the end of growth.
rows.outcome_note: Gordon's stagnation thesis is debated; productivity remained low but AI may yet change the trajectory.

rows.quote: The great stagnation: how America ate all the low-hanging fruit and got caught in a technological plateau.
rows.outcome_note: Stagnation persisted through 2018; median income stagnation was real.

rows.quote: Race Against the Machine: digital technologies will drive imminent productivity acceleration.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity acceleration did not arrive by 2016.

rows.quote: The Second Machine Age: the productivity boom from automation will arrive by 2020.
rows.outcome_note: Productivity boom did not arrive by 2020; COVID-19 created volatility but no structural shift.

rows.quote: The outlook for U.S. productivity growth: IT investment maturity will restore gains.
Trend breaks, long-run restrictions, and productivity recovery.
Long-term budget outlook assumes 1.1% labor productivity growth.
rows.outcome_note: 1.1% not achieved by 2012; actual growth was lower.

rows.quote: Long-term budget outlook: post-crisis recovery will restore 1.3% productivity growth.
FY2008 budget economic assumptions: 1.4% productivity growth.
U.S. economic prospects: secular stagnation and the difficulty of restoring rapid growth.
Why was Europe left at the station when America's productivity locomotive departed? The IT effect is transitory.
rows.outcome_note: Europe did not catch up; US productivity also slowed after 2004.

rows.quote: Productivity and potential output before, during, and after the Great Recession: the slowdown is structural.
The GDP paradox: growth effects of IT have been overestimated.
Are ideas getting harder to find? Research productivity is declining across the economy.
rows.context: NBER WP 23782 (draft 2013). Declining research productivity thesis.
rows.outcome_note: Research productivity thesis holds; TFP remained low through 2020.

rows.quote: The business value of IT: productivity gains from technology investment will materialize by 2015.
How to compete and grow: a sector guide to productivity. IT will drive the next wave.
rows.outcome_note: Next wave did not arrive by 2015.

rows.quote: IT and productivity: the next wave. Technology investment will restore productivity growth.
The productivity dividend: IT investment will pay off in higher productivity growth.
A future that works: AI, automation, employment, and productivity. AI could deliver $13 trillion in additional global economic activity by 2030.
Notes from the AI frontier: modeling the impact of AI on the world economy. Potential 1.4% annual productivity boost.
Sizing the prize: AI could contribute up to $15.7 trillion to the global economy by 2030.
AI and productivity: the $15.7 trillion opportunity by 2030.
The productivity impact of AI: machine learning will raise output per worker.
rows.outcome_note: Some sector-level gains visible by 2025 but aggregate ALP remains modest.

rows.quote: The AI revolution: productivity gains from artificial intelligence will arrive by 2025.
rows.outcome_note: Aggregate gains not yet visible by 2025.

rows.quote: Automation, skills use and training: AI will reshape productivity across OECD economies.
rows.outcome_note: Aggregate TFP effects not yet visible.

rows.quote: Artificial Intelligence in Society: AI will drive productivity growth across sectors by 2030.
Digital Economy Outlook: AI adoption will raise labor productivity by 2025.
rows.outcome_note: Aggregate labor productivity effects modest by 2025.

rows.quote: Robots and jobs: evidence from US labor markets. AI's net effect on employment is negative; TFP gains are limited.
rows.context: NBER WP 23285, 2017. Quantitative ceilings on AI productivity gains.
rows.outcome_note: Acemoglu's skepticism about rapid TFP gains remains consistent with data through 2025.

rows.quote: The race between man and machine: implications of technology for growth, factor shares, and employment.
rows.context: American Economic Review, 2018. Reorganization lags matter.

rows.quote: Automation and new tasks: how technology displaces and reinstates labor. Productivity gains are constrained by task restructuring.
rows.outcome_note: Task restructuring lags continue to constrain measured productivity.

rows.quote: Artificial intelligence and the modern productivity paradox: a clash of expectations and statistics. The J-curve means productivity will return, just delayed by reorganization costs.
rows.outcome_note: J-curve delay ongoing; some sector gains visible by 2024 but aggregate ALP remains modest.

rows.quote: GDP-B: accounting for the value of new and free goods in the digital economy. The productivity gain may already be there; we may not be measuring it.
The Turing trap: the promise and peril of human-like artificial intelligence. Productivity gains are contingent on task redesign, not just technology availability.
Is this time different? The opportunities and challenges of artificial intelligence for productivity.
rows.outcome_note: Cautious optimism not yet vindicated in aggregate data.

rows.quote: AI and the economy: the potential for productivity gains is substantial but not automatic.
The age of secular stagnation: what it is and what to do about it. Tech productivity timing remains elusive.
Why are there still so many jobs? The history and future of workplace automation. Skeptic of rapid automation-driven productivity.
rows.outcome_note: Employment remained high but wage growth modest.

rows.quote: The fall of the labor share and the rise of superstar firms. Tech shifts income toward capital, not necessarily productivity.
rows.outcome_note: Labor share fell but productivity gains remained elusive.

rows.quote: Annual Economic Report: AI and productivity — the effects are uncertain and will take time to materialize.
rows.context: BIS Annual Economic Report, 2018. Chapter on AI and productivity.

rows.quote: The future of U.S. economic growth: the IT boom is over and the new normal is slow.
Generative AI could raise global GDP by 7% ($7 trillion) over a 10-year period.
rows.context: Goldman Sachs, 2023. Specific year targets within the report.

rows.quote: The simple macroeconomics of AI: approximately 0.06% per year TFP gain over 10 years.
rows.context: NBER WP 32487, 2024. The skeptic's specific number.

rows.quote: The economic potential of generative AI: $4.2 trillion annual productivity impact by 2040.
Generative AI at work: 14% productivity gain in customer service; implied scaling claims for broader economy.
rows.context: NBER WP 31161, 2023. With Danielle Li and Lindsey Raymond.
rows.outcome_note: Field experiment results are real; scaling to aggregate by 2028 remains unproven.

rows.quote: AI Jobs Barometer: wage premiums and productivity claims from AI adoption.
It is too early to tell whether AI will have a meaningful effect on productivity.
rows.outcome_note: Powell's caution remains the consensus position.

rows.quote: AI productivity gains are expected to materialize within the next five years.
The Fed is monitoring AI for productivity effects but has not observed them in the aggregate data yet.
Long-term budget outlook assumes ~1.0% labor productivity growth, with a modest AI contribution embedded.
Long-term budget outlook: slightly higher assumption with AI contribution explicit in text.
The Anthropic Economic Index reframes the question of when productivity will show up by tracking AI use in the workforce.
rows.context: Anthropic Economic Index, 2025. Not a prediction but a measurement framework.
rows.outcome_note: Measurement framework, not a dated prediction.

rows.quote: OECD AI Outlook: AI will drive productivity growth across member economies by 2030.
AI will boost global productivity and GDP over the medium term.
AI will drive massive productivity gains across the economy by the late 2020s.
Copilot will fundamentally transform knowledge worker productivity within five years.
Why has economic growth slowed? AI will not reverse the secular stagnation.
The impact of AI on productivity: early evidence suggests gains are real but concentrated.
